Yeah, but this thing was barely watchable.  Stopped watching it with almost twenty minutes of run time left.  Granted the action scenes are intentionally goofy, but the final showdown 
at the amusement park was too ridiculous to finish.  Similar to 'Last Blood', or Rambo 5, or whatever it was.  They could have just blasted Hutch in the back when he turned to leave
the warehouse, but just didn't.  The writers must have used Chat GPT for that scene.  This film works if you want some totally mindless action, or you've already had a lobotomy.

I don’t think they used ChatGPT but they used more of the essence of Nobody 2 is more of a family action film and wanted to present that in the most way possible

author: @jonathankozenko
text:
It was predictable ultimately, but I still had a really good time with it -- I liked the little moment where the lackey corrupt-cop was telling his superior corrupt-cop that Hutch had kicked their asses, and was trying to do it through a mouthful of blood and no teeth left.  Also, I liked how this movie had more hand-to-hand combat, and I loved how he impaled the baddie's shoulder with the anchor.  The only real downside is that they got the Director of the immensely brutal and satisfying Action movies "Headshot" and "The Night Comes for Us", but we barely got his level of intensity in the action sequences.  But beyond that the action sequences were still solid, and Bob Odenkirk is still awesome as the ultra-everyman action hero.
The only serious thing I didn't like was how Hutch was working to pay-off the destruction of the money he burned in the first movie, as that moment was intensely satisfying in the first movie, and it's going to be a bit sour now, knowing that that move cost him months of his life later on.

replies.author: @h1d4pj0k0w1
replies.text: I think the reason why this director held back from his usual style in Headshot and The Night Comes For Us is because Nobody 2 marks his first Hollywood project, which means he had to navigate an entirely new environment and adjust himself to it. Back when he made Headshot and The Night Comes For Us in his home country, he basically had full creative freedom to go as wild and brutal as he wanted, pushing the action to extremes without much restriction. But in Nobody 2, even though he was still given a fair amount of input, he had to balance his vision with the expectations of a bigger studio, mainstream appeal, and Hollywood’s way of doing things. That’s why his signature chaos feels a bit toned down here compared to his earlier works

author: @awesomeexpressionexp
text: Especially went to the theatre to watch it to show support for these types of lower-budget old-school movies. It is very good and like Drinker said, very satisfying sundae, even better than I thought what a sequel would be. Bob is still outstanding as the perfect balance of nobody and badass, especially at 63. The writing is solid and does not insult the audience, it doesn't take itself too seriously, the characters are likable, there are even some legit heart-warming moments. Also, it does manage to add a little something to flesh out several relationships. The only weak(er) link is Sharon Stone, whose weird casino scene makes zero sense even in such a movie, but I still respect her for giving her all and embracing the tone of this movie. Would love to see a third one to round out the meal like a strong craft beer.
